questions.description: Outline the main changes that have been brought about to the roles of the various European Union institutions in the EU…
questions.text: Outline the main changes that have been brought about to the roles of the various European Union institutions in the EU law-making by the Lisbon Treaty amendments. Pay particular attention to the changes that have been made to the roles of; - The European Parliament; - The Commission; and - The Council Discuss whether or not, and in what ways, these changes have made the European Union a more democratic organisation.

questions.description: The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available…
questions.text: The Court of Justice in Case 603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgement that: '....in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union Law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order'. How have these principles of 'equivalence' and practical possibility referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law?

questions.description: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the European Court of Justice, setting out in each…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the European Court of Justice, setting out in each case-note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arouse for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Union judicature: - Case C-341/2005, Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareforbundet ea [2007] ECR I-11767; - Cases C-402 and 415/2005, Yassin Abdullah Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v Council and Commission [2008] ECR I-6351; - Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR I-1459; - Case C-370/2012. Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756; - Case C-280/2000, Atlmark Trans GmbH and Regierungsprasidium Magdeburg v Nahverjehrsgesellschaft Altmark FmbH [2003] ECR I-7747 - Case C-459/99, Courage Ltd. v Crehan [2001] ECR I-6297; - Case 112/84, Humblot v Directeur des Service Fiscaux [1985] ECR 1367; - Case C-236/2009, Association Belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I-773;

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on…
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C-50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[...] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, substantial adverse effect on his interests" He was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non-privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing in his UPA Opinion; and Make an assessment of whether these concerns have been fully addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC and what are now contained in Article 263 TFEU and related case law interpreting those changes.

questions.description: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional)… Regulation.
questions.text: The Department of Health has sought to introduce a number of (fictional) measures in recent years in an attempt to reduce sugar consumption in Ireland. In 2012 the Sale of High Sugar Confectionary Regulations banned the use of vending machines in schools, when used to sell chocolate bars and sweets. Following a complaint made by vending machine suppliers based in Germany and Spain, and a chocolate manufacturer based in the UK, the EU Commission informed the Department that this ban should be lifted or infringement proceedings could be started. The 2012 Regulations were consequently amended and a Code of Conduct on the use of vending machines in schools was introduced instead. In 2014 the Sale of Goods (Advertising) Regulations were amended to include a prohibition on the advertising of high-sugar products in cinemas before 6pm. Again, there were complaints from several EU Member States and this restriction has to be lifted. (Fictional) Figures released by the World Health Organisation in January 2017 show that Ireland has the fastest-rising rates of overweightness in the European Union. It also showed that the Irish population are the second biggest consumers of added sugar in Europe. Public outcry ensued and, as a result, the Department of Health issued a (fictional) press statement in February 2017 indication that the Department was working on a Bill, which will include a provision setting the minimum price for soft drinks which contain more than 10g of added sugar per 100ml. The European Non-Alcoholic Beverages Association (ENBA), which represents the interests of soft drink manufacturers and retailers, have approached you about their concerns over this provision of the, yet to be published, Public Health (Sugar Consumption) Bill 2017. Of particular concern to them is the fact that the Court of Justice, in paragraph 49 of its Judgement of 23 December 2015 in Case C-333/14, Scotch Whiskey Association v The Lord Advocate and the Advocate General for Scotland stated that: "[i]t is for the referring court, which alone has available to it all the matters of fact and law pertaining to the circumstances...to determine whether a [less trade-restricting measure could be] capable of protecting human life and health as effectively as [minimum pricing]". The ENBA fear that this will make it easier for the State to justify a national trade-restricting measure, which appears to be contrary to Article 34 TFEU, on the basis that it is necessary for the protection of human health, as potentially permitted by Article 36 TFEU. Advise the ENBA about this, highlighting the history of the case law of the Court of Justice on Article 34 TFEU, the possible exceptions to its application, and the relevance of the Judgement in Scotch Whiskey to the application of the Department of Health's proposal on minimum pricing.

questions.description: Directive 2014/200/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Directive 2014/200/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions (fictional) establishes a number of new requirements for Member States, designed to ensure that only approved moneylenders are allowed to provide financial credit to customers. Article 9(1) of Directive 2014/200 specifically provides that: "[...] the competent authorities shall refuse authorisation to commence the activity of a credit institution where the initial capital is less that EUR 6 million". As an exception to this, Article 9(4) of the Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States may grant authorisation to credit institutions where the initial capital is less than that specified in paragraph 1, subject to the following conditions: the initial capital is no less than EUR 1 million; and the Member State notifies the Commission of their reasons for exercising this option". Article 13 of the Directive provides that the directors of credit institutions should not be paid any bonus in a year where the institution has failed to make a profit in excess of EUR 1 million. Article 60 of Directive 2014/200/EU states that it was to come into effect by 1 August 2014. The Bank of Ballintubber was granted authorisation to operate credit, and other, facilities by the Central Bank of Ireland on 22 September 2014, despite having an initial capital investment of only EUR 3 million. The EU Commission was never notified about this authorisation. The Supervision of Credit Institutions (Amendment) Regulations 2014, transposing Directive 2014/200/EU, were not introduced until 3 December 2014. In January 2017, following a series of poor lending decisions and the award of large bonuses to the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber, which never made any profits, it has now gone into liquidation. Secure Safes Ltd. are owed EUR 238,900 by the Bank of Ballintubber for the supply and installation of timelock safes during 2016. An initial assessment by the liquidator has shown that there is no chance that unsecured creditors like Secure Safes Ltd. will be paid what they are owed. Secure Safes Ltd. has had to make two staff redundant as a result of this non-payment and the future of this limited company is also now in jeopardy. Advise Secure Safes Ltd., by reference to relevant Court of Justice case law, about whether it can rely on the provisions of Directive 2014/200/EU in separate actions that it is bringing before the Irish courts against (i) the State; (ii) the Central Bank of Ireland; and (iii) the directors of the Bank of Ballintubber.

questions.description: Lena moved from Russia to Belgium in 2004, when she was 17 years old. Advise Lena on both of these issues by reference…
questions.text: Lena moved from Russia to Belgium in 2004, when she was 17 years old. The following year she was convicted of several counts of burglary before the Belgian courts and she was sentenced to three months' imprisonment. Following her release from prison in early 2006, Lena met Ivan, a Belgian national, and they had a child together in April 2009, a boy called Georgi, who was born in Brussels. Georgi is a Belgian national. Lena and Ivan, who have never been married, moved to Dublin in 2013. Soon after this, Lena started work as a nutritionist with a chain of heath food and nutrition supplements shops, called 'Healthy Ways'. Although she has worked in several of their stores around Dublin, Lena has never been promoted. During this period three of her male colleagues, only one of which had more experience and better qualifications than Lena, and one of her female colleagues, who had similar experience and qualifications to Lena, were promoted to assistant managerial positions. Almost seventy per cent of the Healthy Ways workforce are female. Lena previously worked as a nutrition advisor in a similar shop in Brussels, where she also passed a series of certificate exams and completed two correspondence courses on nutrition and supplementation. Lena and Ivan separated in 2015 and Ivan has returned to Belgium, where he has since met and got engaged to another woman. Despite being of the opinion that she is regularly overlooked for promotion and advancement in her employment, Lena really likes living in Dublin and Georgi started primary school there in September 2014. Lena has not yet met the requirements for naturalisation and Irish citizenship and her most recent application for permission to remain in Ireland was rejected in January 2017, because of her criminal record, relating to the offences of which she was convicted in Belgium in 2005. Lena has come to you for advice on (i) whether she may be able to appeal the refusal to grant her continued residence in Ireland because of her criminal record; and (ii) whether she should bring a claim against her employers over their failure to promote her, which she feels is based on gender-bias. Advise Lena on both of these issues by reference to relevant EU legislation and Court of Justice case law.

questions.description: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' .COM (2014) 449) makes it clear…
questions.text: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' .COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: "[...] merger control at the EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEC test" But also that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform" How will this review of merger policy affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Mergers Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: Outline, by reference to the relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union Jurisdiction,…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to the relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case law of the Union Jurisdiction, the roles of (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures but the Lisbon Treaty amendments have improved the democratic legitimacy of decision making within the European Union.

questions.description: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case law, discuss how are they used but the…
questions.text: What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case law, discuss how are they used but the Court of Justice to protect individuals and to ensure that the European Union institutions, and the Member States when implementing EU law, act in accordance with the rule of law?

questions.description: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the Court of Justice of the European Union, setting…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any three of the following eight judgements of the Court of Justice of the European Union, setting out in each case note the core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the Courts of Justice, and how those issues were addressed by the said Court in its judgement: (i) Cases C-501, 513, 515 and 519/06, GlaxoSmithKline Services Unlimited and others v Commission [2009] ECR I-9291 (ii) Case C-372/04, Watts v Bedford Primary Care Trust [2006] ECR I-4325 (iii) Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756 (iv) Case 27/76 United Brands Company and United Brands Continental BV v Commission [1978] ECR 207; (v) Case 340/89 Vlassopoulou v Ministerium fur Justiz Bundes und Europaangelegenheiten Baden- Wurttemberg [1991] ECR 2357 (vi) Case C-236/2009 Association Belege des Consommateurs Tes-Achats and others v Council [2011] ECR I-773 (vii) Case C-352/98 Laboratories Pharmaceutiques Bergaderm SA and Groupil v Commission [2000] ECR I-5291 (viii) Case C-104/09 Roca Alvarez [2010] ECR I-8661.

questions.description: Article 5 of (the ficitional) Directive 2013/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise levels at outdoor…
questions.text: Article 5 of (the ficitional) Directive 2013/456/EU on the approximation of maximum permitted noise levels at outdoor music concerts provides that: "all licence holders operating outdoor music concerts must ensure that those working at these events are subject to a maximum noise level of 116 decibels for a maximum continuous period of four hours." Various methods are set out in the Directive for measuring the noise level at these events. There are also a series of definitions set out, which clearly classify security staff as being amongst that who are "working at these events" and there are therefore covered but the protection set out in the Directive. Article 12 of the same Directive further provides that: "member states shall bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with the Directive including as regards compliance by undertakings and other operators concerned by 31 March 2015." Prior to the introduction of Directive 2013/456/EU, maximum permitted noise levels for concerts in Ireland were set out in the (fictional) Environmental Noise Act 1994. This 1994 Act allowed for exposure to a maximum noise level of 125 decibels for a continuous period of up to six hours. Both the 2013 EU Directive, and the 1994 Act, oblige the organisers of concerts to maintain records of the noise levels recorded during these events for five years. Brian was employed on a part time basis as a security guard by Bool Concerts LTD, between January 2014 amd October 2016. He worked at these events for an average of four days per month during this period. He was regularly exposed to noise levels which exceeded 116 decibels. At one heavy metal festival at which Brian was working, held in Limerick on 3 August 2016, the noise level was recorded as having reached 124 decibels. Brian was exposed to this high noise volume for up to five hours on that day. He has been suffering from tinnitus since this date and he is no longer able to work for Cool Concerts Ltd. As a security guard due to the almost constant ringing noise in his ears. Directive 2013/456/EU was not transposed into Irish Law until the introduction of the (fictional) Environmental Noise (Maximum Permitted Levels at Outdoor Events) Regulations in May 2017. The 2017 Irish regulations set out a range of fines that may be imposed, up to a maximum level of €2,000.00 for the most serious breaches. Brian now seeks your advice on whether he can bring a claim for compensation against either (i) Cool Concerts Ltd; or (ii) the State for the non-application and/or non-implementation of the 2013 Directive. Your advice should focus on whether or not, by reference to relevant EU law principles as developed in pertinent case law of the Union judicature, Brian was protected by the provisions of Directive 2013/456/EU on 3 August 2016 and, if so, whether any remedies any be available to him under EU law.

questions.description: Considering both the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard…
questions.text: Considering both the amendments that have been made to the former Article 230(4) EC by Article 263(4) TFEU with regard to certain Union acts and judicial attempts that have been made to reform the test for individual concern in particular, analyse critically whether it has become any easier in 2017 for non-privileged applicants to seek judicial review of EU acts than it was when Advocate General Jacobs delivered his opinion on Case C-50/00, UPA v Council [2002] ECR I-6677. Your answer should include an analysis of the case --law of the Union judicature interpreting Article 263(4) TFEU.

questions.description: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial… Directive.
questions.text: The Irish Government has recently introduced two (fictional) financial charges on wine regardless of where it is produced. The first is an inspection charge, which is imposed on any wine for sale in Ireland to cover the costs of analysing samples to ensure that the grapes used in production where free of black rot disease at the time of production. These inspections have been ordered following the publication of a recent study by the Organic Growers if Ireland, which has suggested that the presence of this fungus could possible have dome detrimental consequences for the health of humans that consume the wine produced from infected grapes. Similar inspections have not been ordered in any other EU member states, However, (fictional) Directive 97/1234 does allow Member States to analyse samples of wine where health protection concern has been identified. The second charge is an additional charge of €1.50 tax on all bottles of wine that are sold by retailers for more that €25.75 per bottle. This is purely a revenue-raising measure. The average retail price per bottle of wine in Ireland is €13.25. There is no significant level of commercial wine production in Ireland. The European Commission has started an investigation into both the (i) the sampling charge and (ii) the additional retail tax. Advise the Government, by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case law of the Union judicature, on the compatibility of both of these charges with Article 30 TREU and Article 110 TFEU.

questions.description: Trim Technologies Ltd have been producing mobile phones at its factory in… Company.
questions.text: Trim Technologies Ltd have been producing mobile phones at its factory in County Meath since 1997. All of the phones that it produces use lithium-ion batteries manufactured with cobalt. It exports its phones to many EU member states, but its biggest markets are Spain and Portugal. In 2016, following ongoing concerns about the ethical and environmental consequences of cobalt mining in parts of Africa, several EU Member States, including Belgium, Luxembourg and Spain, introduced (fictional) domestic measures requiring that any mobile phones sold in their territory must be certified as being produced to minimum standards -- including providing proof that the cobalt used in the production of their batteries was not extracted using child labour or involving dangerous mining conditions. To date no European Union legislation has been introduced that deals with the ethical or environmental consequences of cobalt mining. Trim Technologies Ltd sources its lithium-ion batteries from a company based in Germany. This company is unable to provide any certification that the batteries have been manufactured with components that did not involve child labour, nor can it certify about any of the other working conditions employed in their production. Certification is available, but only after sending the batteries off to a laboratory for analysis where it can be ascertained that the cobalt used came from specific areas where child labour is definitely no allowed. These tests are expensive. Advise Trim Technologies Ltd about the legal implication of these national measures, especially their compatibility with EU Treaty provisions on measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on trade as interpreted by the Union judicature.

questions.description: Hugo moved to Ireland from France in March 2014. Advise Hugo on whether this refusal to consider his application for…
questions.text: Hugo moved to Ireland from France in March 2014. He found employment in a hotel in Kilkenny in Mat 2014. However, the work was seasonal and his employment there was terminated in late September of the dame year. He did not have set of minimum hours of work during this five month period, working anything from 12 to 35 hours per week, depending on how many guests were staying at the hotel. Hugo was then unemployed for several months before the hotel in Kilkenny re-hired him in March 2015. Hugo left his job at the hotel in June 2015 after the manager accused him of stealing money from the cash register -- an accusation Hugo strenuously denies. After another period of unemployment, Hugo applied for a place on a diploma course in catering management in Carlow Catering College. The fees for the two-year course were €2,200.00 per year. Hugo paid these fees during the first year but was unable to afford the fees for the second year of tuition. As a result, the catering college will not release his exam results for the second and final year of the course. Hugo is furious. He has since discovered that students who have lived in Carlow county for the past three years are entitled to apply for a bursary of up to €1,500.00 towards their college fees. Anyone who has worked in the hospitality sector in Ireland for the past year immediately prior to their enrolment at the college is also entitled to apply for this bursary where he or she has been made involuntarily unemployed. Hugo applied for the bursary, which is awarded by Carlow County Council, but this applications was refused on the grounds that he did not meet the conditions for the award. Advise Hugo on whether this refusal to consider his application for the bursary is compatible with EU law on free movement of persons and what his remedies might be.
